title: Architecture > Population: A Research Program for the Inter-Agent Mesoscale
abstract: The thirteen working pieces in this corpus repeat a single methodological move: a phenomenon ordinarily read as moral, cultural, or characterological is rewritten as the equilibrium of a mechanism with a threshold. We state the move as a research program organised around three claims. First, what looks like a property of people is usually a property of a mechanism; the recurrent objects are operators, sufficient statistics, and threshold games. Second, architecture beats population: replace the users while holding the mechanism fixed and the equilibrium is preserved; replace the mechanism and the equilibrium changes. Third, the scale at which most of contemporary subjective experience is decided — the inter-agent mesoscale — is methodologically nearly empty compared to the scales below the human (atoms, cells, neurons) and above (climate, markets-as-aggregate). The marginal return on formalising one previously unmodelled inter-agent mechanism is therefore large. We address the standard reflexive objection (modelling perturbs the modelled system) by distinguishing three modes of impossibility — information-theoretic, reflexive, and conceptual — and showing, with anchors in the corpus, that each admits a different attack.

title: Personality Is Not Who You Are. It Is Your Cost Tensor.
abstract: Standard personality psychology assigns each person a small number of scalar trait scores assumed stable across contexts. We replace this object with a four-dimensional cost tensor c_i ∈ R⁴_{≥0} over the costs of being publicly wrong, ignored, categorised, and rude, and show that observed action follows the simple rule speak ⇔ B_i > ‖c_i‖_1. The cost tensor depends explicitly on social context through a multiplicative operator L_W acting on R⁴. The Big Five emerge as a five-dimensional projection Π_5(c_i) that loses both the context dependence and the action mechanism. Trauma corresponds to a single high-magnitude diagonal operator Λ_trauma with at least one eigenvalue λ_k ≫ 1 that permanently inflates a cost component; therapy is an iterated sequence of contractive inverse operators with eigenvalues less than one. We state a structural identification result: personality change requires an external operator sequence acting on the dominant component, and pure self-decision is insufficient. The framework reproduces every Big Five regularity as a special case while supplying the missing context dependence and the missing intervention mechanism.

title: The Platform Has a Food Chain. You Are In It.
abstract: Every short-video platform produces an attention pool A_0 generated by creators and consumed by a small number of recurring downstream roles. We classify these roles biologically: Whale (creator, source), Remora (sharer, mutualist), Barnacle (hater, commensal), Plankton (lurker, neutral), Parasite (drama aggregator). Each agent extracts a multiplicative share α_i of the upstream attention. The total attention generated by a cascade of N agents is A(A_0) = A_0 Σ_{i=0}^N Π_{j=1}^i α_j(s_j*), where each s_j* is the strategy chosen by agent j to maximise its own extraction. We show the platform-level objective of maximising A is equivalent to maximising the cascade depth, which under the empirical inequality α_hate > α_agree produces conflict-amplifying recommendation as a Nash equilibrium of the cascade. The model gives a clean Lotka–Volterra coexistence between creators and haters, an interior optimum (not maximum) in conflict intensity for the hater agent, and a structural identification of which agents are extractive (parasite) versus load-bearing-but-invisible (plankton). Nobody in the system is irrational. The platform built the food chain.

title: Instagram Reels Is a TikTok Echo: A Stackelberg Imitation Model of Platform Convergence
abstract: Meta launched Reels in August 2020, the same month the United States threatened to ban TikTok. The simultaneity is the equilibrium of a rational imitation strategy. We formalise short-video recommendation as a sequential ranking problem solved by both platforms with structurally identical neural rankers and Upper Confidence Bound exploration, and show that the only material architectural difference is Instagram's social graph, which acts as a low-pass filter on cold-start virality. Modelling viral spread as contagion, the social prior produces a closed-form imitation lag Δτ = β_0^{-1} s^{-1} ln(1/λ) ≈ 2.55 months. A pilot of 8 documented mainstream-crossover trends (2023–2024) yields sample mean 2.19 months, median 2.25 months — within 15% of the theoretical value. We embed the dynamics in a Stackelberg game with TikTok as leader and Meta as follower, prove pure imitation is weakly dominant under supermodularity, and bound follower regret. The model predicts that micro-trends with half-life shorter than Δτ are structurally immune to Reels imitation, and identifies authenticity-driven content as the only persistent moat TikTok holds. We specify a four-prediction replication protocol using TikTok Research API access.

title: Money or Vibe
abstract: A banker thinks the artist is irrational. The artist thinks the banker is empty. Both are correct from inside their own utility function, both wrong about each other, and the reason is structural. We model human preferences as two-asset utility U(M, V) where M is Money Capital (transferable, accumulable, measurable) and V is Vibe Capital (non-transferable, depreciable, context-specific), and show that for two agents whose utility weights α and β differ, no exchange rate ρ between ΔM and ΔV makes both indifferent — the required ρ_A and ρ_B differ by person. The disagreement between Money-People and Vibe-People is not a values disagreement solvable by sharing more information; they live in different optimization spaces. The eternal money-versus-meaning conversation is microeconomic, not moral.

title: Tier Lists as Identity Filters
abstract: A math tier list posted to TikTok ran for two days and pulled comments. Of seven substantive responses, five were identity-positioning rather than ranking discussion. We formalize the observation: for any tier list T constructed by a single author drawn from a population with ≥4 subdisciplines, and at least one item whose importance varies across subdisciplines, the mutual information I(T;A) about the author's identity exceeds I(T;Θ) about ground-truth importance, provided within-subdiscipline tier assignments are more consistent than between-subdiscipline ones. The structure looks objective. The signal is identity broadcast and audience self-sorting. Boundary: the result fails inside a single subdiscipline or for consensus-objective domains.

title: The TikTok Peer Review
abstract: Academic peer review is sequential. Platform comments are parallel. The latency ratio τ_a/τ_p ≈ 3,000 produces a 9,000× speedup at typical parameters. The depth condition requires N_p ≥ 71 substantive commenters when q_a = 0.7 and q_p = 0.05. We compute both quantities and identify the regime in which platform comments substitute for academic peer review. The model predicts cycle times of two to three weeks for early-stage iteration, consistent with the dz-cascade on B1 Burnout (TikTok, April 2026), where a substantive flaw was identified within 24 hours of posting and the model revised within 14 days. The equivalence breaks for replication, longitudinal validation, and any task requiring institutional infrastructure.

title: Burnout Is Not Too Much Work, v2
abstract: v2 fixes three holes in v1: (i) the collapse threshold ρ* is individual, not universal — we replace ρ_burn = 1 with a population threshold distribution; (ii) 'sustained period' becomes a hazard rate B(t) with explicit timescales; (iii) the overhead/output split is observer-dependent because facade maintenance counts as productive output for the firm and as overhead for the person doing it. Under realistic parameters, a person at ρ^P = 1.50 (collapse-imminent in personal accounting) registers as ρ^F = 0.54 on the firm's dashboard — same hour of work, two valid bookkeepings, almost a full unit apart. The observer-relative split explains why firm-level metrics systematically underestimate burnout and why individual interventions outperform organizational ones in expectation.

title: Burnout Is Not Too Much Work
abstract: The standard explanation of burnout is that you have done too much work. This paper recasts burnout as an overhead-to-output energy ratio ρ_burn = E_overhead / E_output, with a collapse at ρ_burn → 1. Under finite-budget free-energy dynamics the fixed point at ρ_burn = 1 is a transcritical bifurcation, stable below and unstable above. The model explains why rest often fails (it lowers E_total but not the ratio) and why the effective intervention is to reduce overhead (meetings, interruptions, facade maintenance, decision count) rather than workload. Thermodynamic and Bayesian formulations are shown to be equivalent via D_KL of task vs. noise against prior.

title: ADHD Is Not a Deficit. It's a Different Attractor.
abstract: ADHD is usually framed as a deficit. Under a dynamical-systems reading, attention is a state in a phase space with multiple stable attractors. Neurotypical brains occupy a basin near the profile Φ = T − R ≈ 0 (balanced transformation rate and filter friction). ADHD brains occupy a distinct basin near Φ ≈ +2.5 with high variance in attentional bandwidth. Under a two-component free-energy generative model (constraint vs. novelty), the steady-state distribution of Φ is bimodal with an unstable saddle between basins. Stimulants shift the attractor geometrically toward the NT basin rather than eliminating any deficit. Bridge equation: Φ = π_T^-1 − π_R^-1. Testable predictions include failure of Hartigan's dip test for unimodality and reaction-time-variance AUC > 0.85 for ADHD vs non-ADHD separation.

title: Why a Bad Post Doesn't Tank Your Account
abstract: A bad post on TikTok or Instagram appears to tank an account. The mechanism is simpler: within ~90 seconds the platform's posterior over creator class converges and the trial is closed. We model Algorithmic Recovery Time as τ_R ≤ −log α / D_KL · τ_trial, bounded by Bayesian convergence under Bernoulli-like engagement trials. For TikTok-typical parameters (α = 0.05, D_KL ≈ 3 bits, τ_trial ≈ 30s), τ_R ≈ 30–120s — matching creator-reported recovery windows. Cross-platform predictions differ by architecture: ~90s on TikTok, 2–4 hours on Instagram Reels, 30–60 minutes on YouTube Shorts. The platform does not punish bad posts; it reallocates attention via cheap Bayesian inference.

title: Why Two Bumble Filters Cut Your Pool by 80 Percent
abstract: Dating-app filters collapse the pool multiplicatively, not additively. For n independent filters with average pass-rate p̄, surviving pool size is N(n) = N_0 · p̄^n. Two typical filters (distance 0.85, age 0.70) eliminate ~40% of the pool; six eliminate 95%+; ten approach statistical zero. This is the curse of dimensionality for compatibility: log N(n) = log N_0 + n · log p̄, linear in filter count with negative slope. Human probability intuition is approximately additive for small n, which is why the collapse feels counterintuitive. The optimal filter count is small (2–4); beyond that, lost pool size outweighs additional selectivity and the 'perfect partner' filter becomes an empty set.

title: Ghosting Isn't Cowardice. It's Math.
abstract: Ghosting is usually framed as cowardice. Under a sequential dyadic exit game, the equilibrium is determined by the Silence-Cost-Ratio σ_R = c_silence / c_response. When σ_R > 1 the subgame-perfect equilibrium is honest exit; when σ_R < 1 it is ghosting; the transition is sharp. σ_R is driven by the recurrence probability p that the parties or their networks meet again. Pre-2010 (small networks, high p): σ_R ≈ 3. On dating apps (p → 0): σ_R ≈ 0. The shift is structural, not generational — replacing the user pool with people from 1995 under the same platform architecture produces the same equilibrium. The fix is mechanism redesign (e.g. Hinge's 'Your Turn' adds algorithmic c_silence) rather than individual moral change.

title: Why One Methyl Group Makes a Drug 1000× Stronger
abstract: Pharmaceutical chemists have a name for it: Magic Methyl. Adding a single CH3 group at exactly the right atom in a drug candidate sometimes increases binding affinity by three orders of magnitude, while at every other position the same modification does almost nothing. We formalize the phenomenon via a Boltzmann bridge equation relating the binding free-energy change to the ratio of partition functions of accessible bound microstates, and identify Sensitivity Sites as positions where this ratio is finite. An information-theoretic reading recasts the bridge as ΔΔG = −kT · I_conformational, where I is the mutual information between methyl presence and bound conformation. The framework explains why rational prediction is hard (the relevant quantity is conformational, not chemical) and suggests AlphaFold-Multimer plus FEP scanning as a tractable attack.

title: Cartel or Creative Destruction? Eigenvector Asymmetry as a Spectral Diagnostic for Schumpeterian Market Structure
abstract: Market-structure diagnostics based on concentration indices conflate two economically opposite regimes: cartel collusion and Schumpeterian innovation-driven monopoly. Both produce high market concentration, yet only the former is welfare-destroying. We introduce the Schumpeterian Eigenvector Asymmetry (A), a scale-invariant statistic derived from the residual price covariance of firms within a market. Given a high spectral concentration, A identifies whether the dominant covariance mode is uniformly distributed across firms (cartel signature) or concentrated on a single innovator (Schumpeterian signature). We characterize the two regimes as distinct attractors in a Landau-type phase space and demonstrate separation on synthetic data with Cohen's d > 70.
